## A FINE-GRAINED ANALYSIS ON DISTRIBUTION SHIFT

## **Olivia Wiles Sven Gowal Florian Stimberg Ira Ktena Krishnamurthy (Dj) Dvijotham Taylan Cemgil** DeepMind, London, UK

_{_ oawiles,sgowal,stimberg,sylvestre,iraktena,taylancemgil _}_ @deepmind.com

dvij@google.com

## ABSTRACT

Robustness to distribution shifts is critical for deploying machine learning models in the real world. Despite this necessity, there has been little work in defining the underlying mechanisms that cause these shifts and evaluating the robustness of algorithms across multiple, different distribution shifts. To this end, we introduce a framework that enables fine-grained analysis of various distribution shifts. We provide a holistic analysis of current state-of-the-art methods by evaluating 19 distinct methods grouped into five categories across both synthetic and real-world datasets. Overall, we train more than 85K models. Our experimental framework can be easily extended to include new methods, shifts, and datasets. We find, unlike previous work (Gulrajani & Lopez-Paz, 2021), that progress has been made over a standard ERM baseline; in particular, pretraining and augmentations (learned or heuristic) offer large gains in many cases. However, the best methods are not consistent over different datasets and shifts. Code is available at github.com/deepmind/distribution_shift_framework.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

If machine learning models are to be ubiquitous in critical applications such as driverless cars (Janai et al., 2020), medical imaging (Erickson et al., 2017), and science (Jumper et al., 2021), it is pivotal to build models that are robust to distribution shifts. Otherwise, models may fail surprisingly in ways that derail trust in the system. For example, Koh et al. (2020); Perone et al. (2019); AlBadawy et al. (2018); Heaven (2020); Castro et al. (2020) find that a model trained on one set of hospitals may not generalise to the imaging conditions of another; Alcorn et al. (2019); Dai & Van Gool (2018) find that a model for driverless cars may not generalise to new lighting conditions or object poses; and Buolamwini & Gebru (2018) find that a model may perform worse on subsets of the distribution, such as different ethnicities, if the training set has an imbalanced distribution. Thus, it is important to understand when we expect a model to generalise and when we do not. This would allow a practitioner to have confidence in the system (e.g. if a model is demonstrated to be robust to the imaging conditions of different hospitals, then it can be deployed in new hospitals with confidence).

While domain generalization is a well studied area, Gulrajani & Lopez-Paz (2021); Schott et al. (2021) have cast doubt on the efficacy of existing methods, raising the question: _has any progress been made in domain generalization over a standard expectation risk minimization (ERM) algorithm?_ Despite these discouraging results, there are many examples that machine learning models _do_ generalise across datasets with different distributions. For example, CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), with well engineered prompts, generalizes to many standard image datasets. Taori et al. (2020) found that models trained on one image dataset generalise to another, albeit with some drop in performance; in particular, higher performing models generalise better. However, there is little understanding and experimentation on _when_ and _why_ models generalise, especially in realistic settings inspired by real-world applications. This begs the following question:

## _robustness of different methods?_

To answer the above question, we present a grounded understanding of robustness to distribution shifts. We draw inspiration from disentanglement literature (see section 6), which aims to separate images into an independent set of factors of variation (or attributes). In brief, we assume the data

is composed of some (possibly extremely large) set of attributes. We expect models, having seen some distribution of values for an attribute, to be able to learn invariance to that attribute and so to generalise to unseen examples of the attribute and different distributions over that attribute. Using a simple example to clarify the setup, assume our data has two attributes (shape and color) among others. Given data with some distribution over the set of possible colors (e.g. red and blue) and the task of predicting shape (e.g. circle or square), we want our model to generalise to unseen colors (e.g. green) or a different distribution of colors (e.g. there are very few red circles in the training set, but the samples at evaluation are uniformly sampled from the set of possible colors and shapes).

Using this framework, we evaluate models across three distribution shifts: _spurious correlation_ , _lowdata drift_ , and _unseen data shift_ (illustrated in figure 1) and two additional conditions (label noise and dataset size). We choose these settings as they arise in the real world and harm generalization performance. Moreover, in our framework, these distribution shifts are the fundamental blocks of building more complex distribution shifts. We additionally evaluate models when there is varying amounts of label noise (as inspired by noise arising from human raters) and when the total size of the train set varies (to understand how models perform as the number of training examples changes). The unique ability of our framework to evaluate fine-grained performance of models across different distribution shifts and under different conditions is of critical importance to analyze methods under a variety of real-world settings. This work makes the following contributions:

- We propose a framework to when and why we expect methods to generalise. We use this framework to define three, real world inspired distribution shifts. We then use this framework to create a systematic evaluation setup across real and synthetic datasets for different distribution shifts. Our evaluation framework is easily extendable to new distribution shifts, datasets, or methods to be evaluated.

- We evaluate and compare 19 different methods (training more than 85K models) in these settings. These methods span the following 5 common approaches: architecture choice, data augmentation, domain generalization, adaptive algorithms, and representation learning. This allows for a direct comparison across different areas in machine learning.

- tive and that domain generalization algorithms do work for certain datasets and distribution shifts. However, there is no easy way to select the best approach a-priori and results are inconsistent over different datasets and attributes, demonstrating there is still much work to be done to improve robustness in real-world settings.

- 2 FRAMEWORK TO EVALUATE GENERALIZATION

In this section we introduce our robustness framework for characterizing distribution shifts in a principled manner. We then relate three common, real world inspired distribution shifts.

## 2.1 LATENT FACTORISATION

We assume a joint distribution _p_ of inputs _**x**_ and corresponding attributes _y_[1] _, y_[2] _, . . . , y[K]_ (denoted as _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) with _y[k] 2_ A _[k]_ where A _[k]_ is a finite set. One of these _K_ attributes is a label of interest, denoted as _y[l]_ (in a mammogram, the label could be cancer/benign and a nuisance attribute _y[i]_ with _i 6_ = _l_ could be the identity of the hospital where the mammogram was taken). Our aim is to build a classifier _f_ that minimizes the risk _R_ . However, in real-world applications, we only have access to a finite set of inputs and attributes of size _n_ . Hence, we minimize the empirical risk _R_[ˆ] instead:

where _L_ is a suitable loss function. Here, all nuisance attributes _y[k]_ with _k_ = _l_ are ignored and we work with samples obtained from the marginal _p_ ( _y[l] ,_ _**x**_ ). In practice, however, due to selection bias or other confounding factors in data collection, we are only able to train and test our models on data collected from two related but distinct distributions: _p_ train _, p_ test. For example, _p_ train and _p_ test may be concentrated on different subsets of hospitals and this discrepancy may result in a distribution shift; for example, hospitals may use different equipment, leading to different staining on their cell

While generalization in the above sense is desirable for machine learning models, it is not clear _why_ a model _f_ trained on data from _p_ train _should_ generalise to _p_ test. It is worth noting that while _p_ train and _p_ test can be different, they are both related to the true distribution _p_ . We take inspiration from disentanglement literature to express this relationship. In particular, that we can view data as being decomposed into an underlying set of factors of variations. We formalise various distribution shifts using a latent variable model for the true data generation process:

where _z_ denotes latent factors. By a simple refactorization, we can write

Thus, the true distribution can be expressed as the product of the marginal distribution of the attributes with a conditional generative model. We assume that distribution shifts arise when a new marginal distribution for the attributes is chosen, such as _p_ ( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) = _p_ train( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) = _p_ test( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ), but otherwise the conditional generative model is shared across all distributions, i.e., we have _p_ test( _y_[1:] _[K] ,_ _**x**_ ) = _p_ test( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) R _p_ ( _**x** |z_ ) _p_ ( _z|y_[1:] _[K]_ ) _dz_ , and similarly for _p_ train.

To provide more context, as a running example, we use the color DSPRITES dataset (Matthey et al., 2017); where in our notation _y_[1] defines the color with A[1] = _{_ red _,_ green _,_ blue _}_ , and _y_[2] defines the shape with A[2] = _{_ ellipse _,_ heart _,_ square _}_ . We can imagine that a data collector (intentionally or implicitly) selects some marginal distribution over attributes _p_ train( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) when training; for example they select mostly blue ellipses and red hearts. This induces a new joint distribution over latent factors and attributes: _p_ train( _z, y_[1:] _[K]_ ) = _p_ ( _z|y_[1:] _[K]_ ) _p_ train( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ). Consequently, during training, we get images with a different joint distribution _p_ train( _**x** , y_[1:] _[K]_ ) = R _p_ ( _**x** |z_ ) _p_ train( _z, y_[1:] _[K]_ ). This similarly applies when collecting data for the test distribution. We focus on common cases of distribution shifts visualized in figure 1; we discuss these in more detail in section 2.2.

The goal of enforcing robustness to distribution shifts is to maintain performance when the data generating distribution _p_ train changes. In other words, we would like to minimize risk on _p, p_ test given _only_ access to _p_ train. We can achieve robustness in the following ways:

- **Weighted resampling.** We can resample the training set using importance weights _W_ ( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) = _p_ ( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) _/p_ train( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ). This means that given the attributes, the _i_ -th data point ( _yi_[1:] _[K] ,_ _**x** i_ ) in the training set is used with probability _W_ ( _yi_[1:] _[K]_ ) _/_[P] _[n] i[0]_ =1 _[W]_[(] _[y] i_[1:] _[0][K]_ ) rather than 1 _/n_ . We refer to this empirical distribution as _p_ reweight. This procedure requires access to the true distribution of attributes _p_ ( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ), so to avoid bias and improve fairness, it is often assumed that all combinations of attributes happen uniformly at random.

- **Data Augmentation** : Alternatively, we can learn a generative model _p_ ˆ( _**x** |y_[1:] _[K]_ ) from the training data that aims to approximate R _p_ ( _**x** |z_ ) _p_ ( _z|y_[1:] _[K]_ ) _dz_ , as the true conditional

## 2.2 DISTRIBUTION SHIFTS

While distribution shifts can happen in a continuum, we consider three types of shifts, inspired by real-world challenges. We discuss these shifts and two additional, real-world inspired conditions.

**Test distribution** _p_ test **.** We assume that the attributes are distributed uniformly: _p_ test( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) = 1 _/_[Q] _i[|]_[A] _[i][|]_[.][This is desirable, as all attributes are represented and a-priori independent.]

**Shift 1: Spurious correlation – Attributes are correlated under** _p_ train **but not** _p_ test **.** Spurious correlation arises in the wild for a number of reasons including capture bias, environmental factors, and geographical bias (Beery et al., 2018; Torralba & Efros, 2011). These spurious correlations lead to surprising results and poor generalization. Therefore, it is important to be able to build models that are robust to such challenges. In our framework, spurious correlation arises when two attributes _y[a]_ , _y[b]_ are correlated at training time, but this is not true of _p_ test, for which attributes are independent: _p_ train( _y[a] |y_[1] _. . . y[b] . . . y[K]_ ) _> p_ train( _y[a] |y_[1] _. . . y[b][−]_[1] _, y[b]_[+1] _. . . y[K]_ ). This is especially problematic when one attribute _y[b]_ is _y[l]_ , the label. Using the running DSPRITES example, shape and color may be correlated and the model may find it easier to predict color. If color is the label, the model will generalise well. However, if the aim is to predict shape, the model’s reliance on color will lead to poor generalization.

**Shift 2: Low-data drift – Attribute values are unevenly distributed under** _p_ train **but not under** _p_ test **.** Low-data drift arises in the wild (e.g. in (Buolamwini & Gebru, 2018) for different ethnicities) when data has not been collected uniformly across different attributes. When deploying models in the wild, it is important to be able to reason and have confidence that the final predictions will

In the framework above, low-data shifts arise when certain values in the set A _[a]_ of an attribute _y[a]_ are sampled with a much smaller probability than in _p_ test: _p_ train( _y[a]_ = _v_ ) _⌧ p_ test( _y[a]_ = _v_ ). Using the DSPRITES example, only a handful of red shapes may be seen at training time, yet in _p_ test all colors are sampled with equal probability.

**Shift 3: Unseen data shift – Some attribute values are unseen under** _p_ train **but are under** _p_ test **.** This is a special case of _shift 2: low-data drift_ which we make explicit due to its important real world applications. Unseen data shift arises when a model, trained in one setting is expected to work in another, disjoint setting. For example: a model trained to classify animals on images at certain times of day should generalise to other times of day. In our framework, _unseen data shift_ arises when some values in the set A _[a]_ of an attribute _y[a]_ are unseen in _p_ train but are in _p_ test:

This is a stronger constraint than in standard out-of-distribution generalization (see section 6), as multiple values for A _[a]_ must be seen under _p_ train, which allows the model to learn invariance to _y[a]_ . In the DSPRITES example, the color red may be unseen at train time but all colors are in _p_ test.

**Discussion.** We choose these sets of shifts as they are the building blocks of more complex distribution shifts. Consider the simplest case of two attributes: the label and a nuisance attribute. If we consider the marginal distribution of the label, it decomposes into two terms: the conditional probability and the probability of a given attribute value: _p_ ( _y[l]_ ) =[P] _y[a][ p]_[(] _[y][l][|][y][a]_[)] _[p]_[(] _[y][a]_[)][.][The][three] shifts we consider control these terms independently: _unseen data shift_ and _low-data drift_ control _p_ ( _y[a]_ ) whereas _spurious correlation_ controls _p_ ( _y[l] |y[a]_ ). The composition of these terms describes any distribution shift for these two variables.

## 2.3 CONDITIONS

**Label noise.** We investigate the change in performance due to noisy information. This can arise when there are disagreements and errors among the labellers (e.g. in medical imaging (Castro et al., ˆ 2020)). We model this as an observed attribute (e.g. the label) being corrupted by noise. _y[i] ⇠ c_ ( _y[i]_ ), where _y[i] 2_ A _[i]_ is the true label, _y_ ˆ _[i] 2_ A _[i]_ the corrupted, observed one, and _c_ the corrupting function.

**Dataset size.** We investigate how performance changes with the size of the training dataset. This setting arises when it is unrealistic or expensive to collect additional data (e.g. in medical imaging or in camera trap imagery). Therefore, it is important to understand how performance degrades given fewer total samples. We do this by limiting the total number of samples from _p_ train.

## 3 MODELS EVALUATED

We evaluate 19 algorithms to cover a broad range of approaches that can be used to improve model robustness to distribution shifts and demonstrate how they relate to the three ways to achieve robustness, outlined in section 2. We believe this is the first paper to comprehensively evaluate a large set of different approaches in a variety of settings. These algorithms cover the following areas: architecture choice, data augmentation, domain adaptation, adaptive approaches and representation learning. Further discussion on how these models relate to our robustness framework is in appendix E.

**Architecture choice.** We evaluate the following standard vision models: ResNet18, ResNet50, ResNet101 (He et al., 2016), ViT (Dosovitskiy et al., 2021), and an MLP (Vapnik, 1992). We use weighted resampling _p_ reweight to oversample from the parts of the distribution that have a lower probability of being sampled from under _p_ train. Performance depends on how robust the learned representation is to distribution shift.

**Heuristic data augmentation.** These approaches attempt to approximate the true underlying generative model _p_ ( _**x** |y_[1:] _[K]_ ) in order to improve robustness. We analyze the following augmentation methods: standard ImageNet augmentation (He et al., 2016), AugMix without JSD (Hendrycks et al., 2020), RandAugment (Cubuk et al., 2020), and AutoAugment (Cubuk et al., 2019). Performance depends on how well the heuristic augmentations approximate the true generative model.

**Learned data augmentation.** These approaches approximate the true underlying generative model _p_ ( _**x** |y_[1:] _[K]_ ) by learning augmentations conditioned on the nuisance attribute. The learned augmentations can be used to transform any image _**x**_ to have a new attribute, while keeping the other attributes fixed. We follow Goel et al. (2020), who use CYCLEGAN (Zhu et al., 2017), but we do not use their SGDRO objective in order to evaluate the performance of learned data augmentation alone. Performance depends on how well the learned augmentations approximate the true generative model.

**Domain generalization.** These approaches aim to recover a representation _z_ that is independent of the attribute: _p_ ( _y[a] , z_ ) = _p_ ( _y[a]_ ) _p_ ( _z_ ) to allow generalization over that attribute. We evaluate IRM (Arjovsky et al., 2019), DeepCORAL (Sun & Saenko, 2016), domain MixUp (Gulrajani & LopezPaz, 2021), DANN (Ganin et al., 2016), and SagNet (Nam et al., 2021). Performance depends on the invariance of the learned representation _z_ .
**Adaptive approaches.** These works modify _p_ reweight dynamically. We evaluate JTT (Liu et al., 2021) and BN-Adapt (Schneider et al., 2020). These methods do not give performance guarantees.

**Representation learning.** These works aim to learn a robust representation of _z_ that describes the true prior. We evaluate using a _β_ -VAE (Higgins et al., 2017a) and pretraining on ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009). Performance depends on the quality of the learned representation for the specific task.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

We evaluate the 19 different methods across these six datasets, three distribution shifts, varying label noise, and dataset size. We plot aggregate results in figures 3-7 and complete results in the appendix in figures 10-12. We discuss the results by distilling them into seven concrete takeaways in section 4.1 and four practical tips in section 4.2.

**Datasets.** DSPRITES (Matthey et al., 2017), MPI3D (Gondal et al., 2019), SMALLNORB (LeCun et al., 2004), SHAPES3D (Burgess & Kim, 2018), CAMELYON17 (Koh et al., 2020; Bandi et al., 2018), and IWILDCAM (Koh et al., 2020; Beery et al., 2018). These datasets consist of multiple (potentially an arbitrarily large number) attributes. We select two attributes _y[l] , y[a]_ for each dataset and make one _y[l]_ the label. We then use these two attributes to build the three shifts. Visualizations of samples from the datasets are given in figure 2 and further description in appendix D.1. We discuss precisely how we set up the shifts, choose the attributes, and additional conditions for these datasets in appendix D.2.

**Model selection.** When investigating heuristic data augmentation, domain generalization, learned augmentation, adaptive approaches, and representation learning, we use a ResNet18 for the simpler, synthetic datasets (DSPRITES, MPI3D, SHAPES3D, and SMALLNORB) but a ResNet50 for the

## 4.1 TAKEAWAYS

**Takeaway 1: While we can improve over ERM, no one method always performs best.** The relative performance between methods varies across datasets and shifts. Under _spurious correlation_ (figure 3), CYCLEGAN consistently performs best but in figure 4, under _low-data drift_ , pretraining consistently performs best. Under _unseen data shift_ (figure 5), pretraining is again one of the best performing models. However, if we drill down on the results in figure 10 (appendix B.1), we can see pretraining performs best on the synthetic datasets, but not on CAMELYON17 (where using augmentation or DANN is best) or IWILDCAM (where using ViT or an MLP is best).

**Takeaway 2: Pretraining is a powerful tool across different shifts and datasets.** While pretraining is not always helpful (e.g. in appendix B.1 on CAMELYON17 in figures 10-11, IWILDCAM in figures 10-11), it often provides a strong boost in performance. This is presumably because the representation _z_ learned during pretraining is helpful for the downstream task. For example, the representation may have been trained to be invariant to certain useful properties (e.g. scale, shift, and color). If these properties are useful on the downstream tasks, then the learned representation should improve generalization.

**Takeaway 3: Heuristic augmentation improves generalization** _**if**_ **the augmentation describes an attribute.** In all settings (figures 3-5), ImageNet augmentation generally improves performance. However, RandAugment, AugMix, and AutoAugment have more variable performance (as further shown in figures 10-12). These methods are compositions of different augmentations. We investigate the impact of each augmentation in RandAugment in appendix B.2 and find variable performance. Augmentations that approximate the true underlying generative model _p_ ( _**x** |y_[1:] _[K]_ ) lead to the best results; otherwise, the model may waste capacity. For example, on CAMELYON17 (which consists of cell images), color jitter harms performance but on SHAPES3D and MPI3D it is essential.

**Takeaway 4: Learned data augmentation is effective across different conditions and distribution shifts.** This approach is highly effective in the _spurious correlation_ setting (figure 3). It can also help in the _low-data_ and _unseen data shift_ settings (figure 4,5) (though the gains for these two shifts are not as large as for pretraining). The effectiveness of this approach can be explained by the fact that if the augmentations are learned perfectly, then augmented samples by design are from the true underlying generative model and can cover missing parts of the distribution.

**Takeaway 5: Domain generalization algorithms offer limited performance improvement.** In some cases these methods (in particular DANN) do improve performance, most notably in the _lowdata drift_ and _unseen data shift_ settings (figures 4-5). However, this depends on the dataset (see figures 10-12) and performance is rarely much better than using heuristic augmentation.

**Takeaway 7: The precise attributes we consider directly impacts the results.** For example, on DSPRITES, if we make color _y[l]_ and shape _y[a]_ , we find that _all_ methods generalise perfectly in the _unseen data shift_ setting (as demonstrated in appendix B.3) unlike when shape is _y[l]_ (figure 10).

## 4.2 PRACTICAL TIPS

While there is no free lunch in terms of the method to choose, we recommend the following tips.

**Tip 1: If heuristic augmentations approximate part of the true underlying generative model, use them.** Under this constraint, heuristic augmentations can significantly improve performance; this should be a first point of call. How to heuristically choose these augmentations without exhaustively trying all possible combinations is an open research question.

**Tip 2: If heuristic augmentations do not help, learn the augmentation.** If the true underlying generative model cannot be readily approximated with heuristic techniques, but some subset of the generative model can be learned by conditioning on known attributes, this is a promising way to further improve performance. How to learn the underlying generative model directly from data and use this for augmentation is a promising area to explore more thoroughly.

**Tip 3: Use pretraining.** In general, pretraining was found to be a useful way to learn a robust representation. While this was not true for all datasets (e.g. CAMELYON17, IWILDCAM), performance could be dramatically improved by pretraining (DSPRITES, MPI3D, SMALLNORB, SHAPES3D). An area to be investigated is the utility of self-supervised pre-training.

**Tip 4: More complex approaches lead to limited improvements.** Domain generalization, adaptive approaches and disentangling lead to limited improvements, if any, across the different datasets and shifts. Of these approaches, DANN performs generally best. How to make these approaches generically useful for robustness is still an open research question.

## 5 DISCUSSION

Our experiments demonstrate that no one method performs best over all shifts and that performance is dependent on the precise attribute being considered. This leads to the following considerations.

**There is no way to decide a-priori on the best method given only the dataset.** It would be helpful for practitioners to be able to select the best approaches without requiring comprehensive evaluations and comparisons. Moreover, it is unclear how to pinpoint the precise distribution shift (and thereby methods to explore) in a given application. This should be an important future area of investigation.
**We should focus on the cases where we have knowledge about the distribution shift.** We found that the ability of a given algorithm to generalize depends heavily on the attribute and dataset being

8

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

considered. Instead of trying to make one algorithm for any possible shift, it makes sense to have adaptable algorithms which can use auxiliary information if given. Moreover, algorithms should be evaluated in the context for which we will use them.

**It is pivotal to evaluate methods in a variety of conditions.** Performance varies due to the number of examples, amount of noise, and size of the dataset. Thus it is important to perform comprehensive evaluations when comparing different methods, as in our framework. This gives others a more realistic view of different models’ relative performance in practice.

## 6 RELATED WORK

C.

**Benchmarking robustness to out of distribution (OOD) generalization.** While a multitude of methods exist that report improved OOD generalization, Gulrajani & Lopez-Paz (2021) found that in actuality no evaluated method performed significantly better than a strong ERM baseline on a variety of datasets. However, Hendrycks et al. (2021) found that, when we focus on better augmentation, larger models and pretraining, we can get a sizeable boost in performance. This can be seen on the Koh et al. (2020) benchmark (the largest boosts come from larger models and better augmentation). Our work is complementary to these methods, as we look at a range of approaches (pretraining, heuristic augmentation, learned augmentation, domain generalisation, adaptive, disentangled representations) on a range of both synthetic and real-world datasets. Moreover, we allow for a fine-grained analysis of methods over different distribution shifts.

**Benchmarking spurious correlation and low-data drift.** Studies on fairness and bias (surveyed by Mehrabi et al. (2021)) have demonstrated the pernicious impact of low-data in face recognition (Buolamwini & Gebru, 2018), medical imaging (Castro et al., 2020), and conservation (Beery et al., 2018) and spurious correlation in classification (Geirhos et al., 2019) and conservation (Beery et al., 2020). Arjovsky et al. (2019) hypothesized that spurious correlation may be the underlying reason for poor generalization of models to unseen data. To our knowledge, there has been no large scale work focused on understanding the benefits of different methods across these distribution shifts systematically across multiple datasets and with fine-grained control on the amount of shift. Here we introduce a framework for creating these shifts in a controllable way to allow such challenges to be investigated robustly.

**Benchmarking disentangled representations.** A related area, disentangled representation learning, aims to learn a representation where the factors of variation in the data are separated. If this could be achieved, then models should be able to generalise effortlessly to unseen data as investigated in multiple settings such as reinforcement learning (Higgins et al., 2017b). Despite many years of work in disentangled representations (Higgins et al., 2017a; Burgess et al., 2017; Kim & Mnih, 2018; Chen et al., 2018), a benchmark study by Locatello et al. (2019) found that, without supervision or implicit model or data assumptions, one cannot reliably perform disentanglement; however, weak supervision appears sufficient to do so (Locatello et al., 2020). Dittadi et al. (2021); Schott et al. (2021); Montero et al. (2020) further investigated whether representations (disentangled or not) can interpolate, extrapolate, or compose properties; they found that when considering complex combinations of properties and multiple datasets, representations do not do so reliably.

## 7 CONCLUSIONS

This work has put forward a general, comprehensive framework to reason about distribution shifts. We analyzed 19 different methods, spanning a range of techniques, over three distribution shifts – _spurious correlation_ , _low-data drift_ , and _unseen data shift_ , and two additional conditions – _label noise_ and _dataset size_ . We found that while results are not consistent across datasets and methods, a number of methods do better than an ERM baseline in some settings. We then put forward a number of practical tips, promising directions, and open research questions. We hope that our framework and comprehensive benchmark spurs research on in this area and provides a useful tool for practitioners to evaluate which methods work best under which conditions and shifts.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a comprehensive and ambitious effort to systematically analyze model robustness under distribution shift through a fine-grained, extensible experimental framework. The writing is generally clear, and the motivation—highlighting the critical need for robustness in real-world applications—is well-grounded and timely. The authors make a strong contribution by evaluating over 19 methods across multiple categories (e.g., data augmentation, domain generalization) and over 85K trained models, which lends significant empirical weight to their conclusions. Their re-evaluation of progress relative to ERM challenges prior pessimistic narratives, identifying meaningful improvements from pretraining and augmentation strategies—results that are both practically and theoretically important. However, a major limitation lies in the lack of methodological detail in the provided text: key aspects such as how the distribution shifts are concretely implemented, how attributes are defined or disentangled in real data, and what constitutes the "synthetic" datasets remain underspecified, making it difficult to assess the validity and generalizability of the findings. Additionally, while the latent factorization framework draws inspiration from disentanglement, the transition from conceptual model to empirical evaluation is under-explained, leaving ambiguous how precisely the assumed independence of attributes holds in practice. The absence of a formal definition for the three core shift types—spurious correlation, low-data drift, unseen data shift—beyond brief descriptions also weakens the theoretical foundation. For a paper claiming to offer a *framework*, greater exposition on the design principles and assumptions behind the experimental setup is warranted. Overall, the scope and scale of the empirical analysis suggest high potential impact, but the clarity and rigor of the methodological presentation would benefit from substantial strengthening.

Given that domain generalization methods like IRM and DANN are highly sensitive to hyperparameters, and adaptive methods rely on specific implementation choices, the lack of transparency raises concerns about evaluation fairness. If DANN consistently outperforms ERM, 'limited improvements' understates its value; if it barely beats other failures, the phrasing is fair. Either way, the ambiguity risks over-pessimism. comments.title: Inaccurate and misleading terminology in representation learning discussion

The shared goal is learning transferable, disentangled features—not recovering a ground-truth p(z). Referring to this as the 'true prior' is technically inaccurate and may mislead readers into thinking these methods recover veridical latent structure in a formal Bayesian sense. More precise language (e.g., 'robust' or 'invariant representation') would avoid over-unifying conceptually distinct approaches.

The lack of quantification makes it difficult to operationalize: how much smaller must the probability be to qualify as 'low-data drift'? Is it relative or absolute? While the accompanying example (DSPRITES with 'a handful of red shapes') gives intuition, the formal definition should not rely on prose to fill this gap. In a rigorous evaluation framework—especially one aiming to be extendable and systematic—the criteria for what constitutes a shift should be quantitatively specified. Without this, replicating or extending the experimental setup requires guesswork. The issue is mitigated by the fact that the experiments in Figures 3 and 4 control _N_ explicitly, so the implementation is likely precise. comments.quote: This is a special case of _shift 2: low-data drift_ which we make explicit due to its important real world applications. comments.explanation: The definition ends with a colon and no equation or formal condition—it cuts off mid-sentence. This suggests either OCR failure or an incomplete edit. The prose says 'are unseen in _p_train_ but are in _p_test_', which implies _p_train(y[a] = v) = 0_ while _p_test(y[a] = v) > 0_. That is the standard definition of unseen (or out-of-support) shifts. Moreover, the claim that this is a 'special case' of low-data drift is accurate only if one interprets 'low' as including zero—yet in practice, low-data and unseen are often treated differently (e.g., augmentation can't help if no examples exist). The surrounding text notes that 'multiple values for A[a] must be seen under _p_train_', which provides useful context (ruling out fully OOD), but the lack of a formal expression weakens the precision. As this is a core shift type, a clear mathematical definition should be provided. Given that the paper aims for systematic evaluation, this is more than a stylistic issue—it affects reproducibility.

A more correct phrasing would be to say that _p_train( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) and _p_test( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) are chosen differently from _p_, not that they are equal to each other or to _p_. This misuse risks misleading a careful reader about whether the framework assumes identical marginals. comments.explanation: The denominator suggests a sum over indices from i[0] = 1 to n, but 'i[0]' is nonstandard and likely an OCR artifact for 'i=1'. The expression is meant to represent the normalized probability: _W(y_i) / ∑_{j=1}^n W(y_j). The numerator uses _yi_[1:] _[K]_, while the denominator uses _[y] i_[1:] _[0][K]_—the '0' is inconsistent. This appears to be another OCR error where brackets were misparsed. Context from importance weighting and sampling theory confirms the intent: importance weights are normalized to define a new empirical distribution. Given common practice in resampling, the interpretation is recoverable, but a reader relying solely on the text might be briefly confused by the malformed indices.

comments.title: Incorrect assumption in representation learning factorization
comments.quote: An alternative factorization of a data generating distribution (e.g. train) is _p_ train( _y_[1:] _[K] ,_ _**x**_ ) = R _p_ ( _z|_ _**x**_ ) _p_ train( _y_[1:] _[K] |z_ ) _dz_ .
comments.explanation: Initially, this raises concern because the standard forward generative process is _p_ ( **x** | z), not inference in reverse. Here, the authors propose modeling _p_ train(y, **x**) via marginalization over _p_ (z | **x**) and _p_ (y | z), which corresponds to an inference (encoding) model rather than the generative one. While technically valid as a decomposition, this choice is unusual for defining a data-generating process and may confuse readers. The issue is compounded by the following claim: '_p_ ( _z|_ _**x**_ ) _/ p_ ( _**x** |z_ ) _p_ ( _z_ )'—which seems to mean proportionality via Bayes’ rule—but is imprecisely stated. What is likely intended is that under a shared prior _p_ (z), the encoder _p_ (z | **x**) adapts to the data distribution, but robustness stems from downstream classifier invariance. The idea aligns with VAEs and self-supervised learning, so the intent is sound, but the formal expression could mislead a reader into thinking the true data distribution is defined backward via inference. A more accurate phrasing would clarify that _p_ (z | **x**) is learned, not assumed as part of the true data-generating process.

comments.title: Misleading implication about access to true attribute distribution
comments.quote: This procedure requires access to the true distribution of attributes _p_ ( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ), so to avoid bias and improve fairness, it is often assumed that all combinations of attributes happen uniformly at random.
comments.explanation: The text states that weighted resampling requires access to the true _p_ (y_[1:]_[K]_), but then suggests in practice we assume uniformity. This creates tension: if we assume uniformity regardless, we are not using the true _p_, but a hypothetical one. This undermines the stated precondition. In reality, reweighting typically assumes a target distribution (like uniform) rather than actual knowledge of the true _p_. The authors likely mean 'target' or 'desired' distribution, not 'true' in the data-generating sense. However, using 'true distribution' here conflates ontological and normative concepts—this is a subtle but important distinction, especially in fairness contexts. While domain experts would recognize the assumption, a broader audience might overestimate what is required for reweighting. The claim thus slightly overstates practical requirements.

comments.title: Inconsistent indexing in running example
comments.quote: where in our notation _y_[1] defines the color with A[1] = _{_ red _,_ green _,_ blue _}_ , and _y_[2] defines the shape with A[2] = _{_ ellipse _,_ heart _,_ square _}_
comments.explanation: The indexing here uses _y_[1] and _y_[2], but elsewhere the paper uses _y_l for the label and _y_a for a nuisance attribute. In the DSPRITES example, if shape is the label of interest, then _l=2_, and the text is consistent in this regard. No contradiction is found upon checking: the example doesn’t conflict with the general definition. The notation is used consistently, with subscripts for attribute index and _l_ as the special index for the label. This appears correct upon closer inspection.

comments.title: Underdescription of augmentation model learning
comments.quote: we can learn a generative model _p_ ˆ( _**x** |y_[1:] _[K]_ ) from the training data that aims to approximate R _p_ ( _**x** |z_ ) _p_ ( _z|y_[1:] _[K]_ ) _dz_
comments.explanation: The text claims we can learn _p_hat(**x** | y_[1:]_[K])_ to approximate the true conditional, but does not specify how. In particular, from training data alone, learning a full generative model conditioned on all attributes is underspecified—this requires either strong inductive biases (e.g. in cycle-consistent GANs) or access to labeled attributes. While later text references CYCLEGAN and learned augmentations, the general claim here oversimplifies the feasibility. Without assumptions about the latent space or disentanglement, this approximation may not be identifiable from marginal data. The authors correctly link it to later methods (like CYCLEGAN), so the framework is not invalid, but as stated, this theoretical premise slightly underdescribes the identifiability challenges, which could mislead a reader into thinking such models are generally learnable from unlabeled data. This is a know issue in representation learning, so omitting it is a mild oversight.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'true prior' in representation learning
comments.quote: These works aim to learn a robust representation of _z_ that describes the true prior.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the phrase 'true prior' in the context of representation learning appears vague or potentially misleading. In standard variational inference or VAE frameworks, the 'prior' typically refers to p(z), a distribution over latent variables assumed during generative modeling. However, here the sentence suggests that learning a representation z that 'describes the true prior' is a goal of β-VAE and ImageNet pretraining. This could mislead a reader into thinking that these methods are explicitly modeling p(z) as a Bayesian prior in a generative sense. Upon reviewing the surrounding context—particularly section 2.1, which discusses latent factorisation and conditional generation p(x|z)—it becomes clearer that the authors are referring more broadly to learning a representation z that reflects invariant, underlying structure of the data (i.e., a disentangled or robust latent space), rather than strictly enforcing a specific p(z). Still, the term 'true prior' is nonstandard in this context and conflates two ideas: (1) the prior over latents, and (2) the idea of a 'veridical' or ground-truth factorization of variation. While β-VAE does encourage disentanglement by modifying the prior (often taking it as isotropic normal), pretraining on ImageNet does not explicitly model any prior over latents. Thus, the commonality between β-VAE and ImageNet pretraining lies in learning useful, transferable representations—not in recovering a 'true prior' in the formal Bayesian sense. This phrasing risks over-unifying two conceptually distinct approaches under a term that is technically inaccurate.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for datasets in IWILDCAM citation
comments.quote: and IWILDCAM (Koh et al., 2020; Beery et al., 2018)
comments.explanation: The citation for IWILDCAM lists Koh et al., 2020 alongside Beery et al., 2018. However, from prior context (and general knowledge in the field), IWILDCAM is introduced in Beery et al., 2018 (iWildCam challenge), while Koh et al., 2020 is associated with CAMELYON17. This raises an immediate concern about a citation error. But checking the full passage, the same citation pattern appears earlier: 'CAMELYON17 (Koh et al., 2020; Bandi et al., 2018), and IWILDCAM (Koh et al., 2020; Beery et al., 2018)'. The repetition of Koh et al., 2020 for both datasets suggests either a copy-paste error or a misattribution. However, this may also stem from OCR issues: perhaps the original text had 'Beery et al. (2018)' only for IWILDCAM, but OCR misaligned the parentheses. Given that Koh et al. (2020) is definitively linked to CAMELYON17 (as confirmed in the paper’s own context) and not IWILDCAM, citing it for the latter is substantively incorrect. The error persists across two instances and is not resolved by context. This is not just a formatting issue—it misattributes foundational work and could mislead readers about the provenance of the dataset.

comments.title: Unclear model selection procedure for adaptive and domain generalization methods
comments.quote: When investigating heuristic data augmentation, domain generalization, learned augmentation, adaptive approaches, and representation learning, we use a ResNet18 for the simpler, synthetic datasets... but a ResNet50 for the more complex, real world ones... We perform a sweep over the hyperparameters... To choose the best model for each seed, we perform model selection over _all_ hyperparameters using the top-1 accuracy on the validation set.
comments.explanation: The model selection procedure is described at a high level, but critical details are missing. Specifically, it's unclear whether hyperparameter sweeps were conducted separately for each method (e.g., for IRM, DANN, CYCLEGAN, etc.) and what those hyperparameters were. For instance, domain generalization methods like IRM or DANN have specific hyperparameters (e.g., penalty strength, number of training environments) that are crucial to performance. Similarly, adaptive approaches like BN-Adapt involve choices about batch normalization statistics or adaptation steps. The claim that model selection is done 'over all hyperparameters' using the validation set is underspecified: does this include method-specific hyperparameters? How were they bounded? The paper states sweeps are 'given in appendix F.8', but the passage we are reviewing does not include that. As a result, a reader cannot determine whether fair or optimal configurations were used for each method. This lack of specification affects reproducibility and raises concerns about whether the comparisons in figures 3–7 are truly balanced across methods. While the approach is principled in intent, the omission of even a summary of how method-specific tuning was handled makes it difficult to trust the fairness of the evaluation, especially given that some methods (e.g., IRM) are known to be sensitive to hyperparameter choices.

comments.title: Overstatement of generality in takeaway about learned data augmentation
comments.quote: Takeaway 4: Learned data augmentation is effective across different conditions and distribution shifts. This approach is highly effective in the _spurious correlation_ setting (figure 3). It can also help in the _low-data_ and _unseen data shift_ settings (figure 4,5)...
comments.explanation: The takeaway claims broad effectiveness of learned data augmentation (specifically CYCLEGAN) across shifts, supported by figures 3–5. While the data in figure 3 shows CYCLEGAN performing best under spurious correlation, figures 4 and 5 show more mixed results: under low-data drift (fig 4), pretraining dominates, and CYCLEGAN is only marginally above baseline; under unseen shift (fig 5), it ranks around median. The term 'can also help' is accurate, but the framing as 'effective across different conditions' overreaches. Moreover, figure 10 (appendix) likely reveals dataset-specific performance—e.g., CYCLEGAN may not generalize well to medical images (CAMELYON17) due to domain-specific textures. The claim would be stronger if it acknowledged that effectiveness heavily depends on the ability to learn meaningful, attribute-preserving transforms (e.g., color/shape transfer in synthetic data), which may fail on real-world textures or high-fidelity medical images. As written, the takeaway could mislead a practitioner into thinking learned augmentation is reliably beneficial, when in fact it may be brittle outside controlled settings. The evidence supports a more nuanced view.

comments.title: Ambiguity in handling of OOD validation set for unseen data shift
comments.quote: In the _unseen data shift_ setting for the CAMELYON17 and IWILDCAM, we use the given out-of-distribution validation set, which is a different, distinct set in _D_ that is independent of _D_ train _, D_ test.
comments.explanation: This statement raises a red flag: if the validation set is out-of-distribution (unseen attribute values), then using it for model selection based on top-1 accuracy assumes access to labels from the target distribution, which contradicts the typical assumption in unseen data shift scenarios where such data is unavailable at training time. However, the authors clarify this is a validation set 'given'—suggesting it's provided as part of the benchmark. In practice, some datasets (e.g., CAMELYON17-WILDS) do provide such an auxiliary OOD set for model selection. So while this is a real concern, the context resolves it: the setup assumes oracle access to an OOD validation set for model tuning, which is a known but strong assumption. Still, the phrasing 'independent of D_train, D_test' is ambiguous—does it mean independently sampled, or from a different domain entirely? Without clarification, a reader might assume the validation set contains the same kind of unseen values as the test set, which would be necessary for meaningful selection. The method is justifiable within standard WILDS-style evaluation protocols, but the description is underspecified regarding the nature and representativeness of this validation set.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of Attribute Correlation in Spurious Correlation Shift
comments.quote: Spurious correlation arises when two attributes $ y[a] $, $ y[b] $ are correlated at training time, but this is not true of $ p_\text{test} $, for which attributes are independent: $ p_\text{train}( y[a] |y_{1} \ldots y[b] \ldots y[K] ) > p_\text{train}( y[a] |y_{1} \ldots y[b]^{-1}, y[b]^{+1} \ldots y[K] ) $.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned that the inequality involving conditioning on $ y[b]^{-1} $ and $ y[b]^{+1} $ was not standard notation and possibly a misrendering due to OCR or incorrect indexing. The expression $ y[b]^{-1}, y[b]^{+1} $ does not align with standard conditioning notation and appears to be an artifact—perhaps intended to denote exclusion of $ y[b] $, which would normally be written $ \setminus y[b] $ or via explicit set notation. Given the surrounding context, especially the prose clarifying that spurious correlation involves dependence in $ p_\text{train} $ but independence in $ p_\text{test} $, the authors’ intent is clear: they aim to say that conditioning on $ y[b] $ changes the distribution of $ y[a] $, indicating correlation. However, the mathematical expression is malformed. A correct formulation would compare $ p_\text{train}(y[a] \mid \mathbf{y}_{\setminus b}) $ and $ p_\text{train}(y[a] \mid \mathbf{y}_{\setminus b}, y[b]) $, or state that the joint is not equal to the product of marginals. Even assuming the best OCR correction, the current version fails to express this cleanly. This could mislead a careful reader expecting formal rigor, especially in a section defining key concepts.

comments.title: Confusing Reference to SGDRO Objective in Learned Augmentation Section
comments.quote: We follow Goel et al. (2020), who use CYCLEGAN (Zhu et al., 2017), but we do not use their SGDRO objective in order to evaluate the performance of learned data augmentation alone.
comments.explanation: At first, I wondered whether the omission of the SGDRO objective was clearly justified or explained. Goel et al. (2020) introduced SGDRO as part of a domain-robust training procedure combining learned augmentation with reweighting. By excluding it, the authors aim to isolate learned data augmentation—this intent is reasonable and appropriate for their goal. However, the phrase 'we do not use their SGDRO objective' provides no context about what SGDRO is or why its removal isolates augmentation. A reader unfamiliar with SGDRO may not know whether this change substantively alters the method. While the broader goal (evaluating augmentation in isolation) is sound, the explanation is underspecified. Given that SGDRO involves both reweighting and optimization dynamics specific to domain shifts, simply naming it without clarification leaves the actual implementation of the learned augmentation method underspecified. Reproducing the exact procedure would require external knowledge.

comments.title: Overstatement of Pretraining Benefit in Tip 3
comments.quote: While this was not true for all datasets (e.g. CAMELYON17, IWILDCAM), performance could be dramatically improved by pretraining (DSPRITES, MPI3D, SMALLNORB, SHAPES3D).
comments.explanation: The claim that pretraining leads to 'dramatically improved' performance appears strong. However, from figures 3–4 (and the summary), the benefit varies significantly. In CAMELYON17 and IWILDCAM—real-world biomedical and ecological datasets—pretraining shows little or negative gain, which is crucial context. The use of 'dramatically' relative to synthetic datasets (DSPRITES, etc.), where gains are more common, risks overstating general effectiveness. While the authors do qualify the claim with 'not true for all datasets', the juxtaposition of 'dramatically improved' with only two negative examples weakens the precision of the conclusion. In a balanced interpretation, pretraining helps in controlled, synthetic, or object-centric domains but not necessarily in complex real-world medical or ecological ones. Thus, the claim leans toward being overly optimistic without emphasizing the domain limitations.

comments.title: Misleading Impression from Tip 4 on Domain Generalization Methods
comments.quote: Domain generalization, adaptive approaches and disentangling lead to limited improvements, if any, across the different datasets and shifts. Of these approaches, DANN performs generally best.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this tip seems consistent with the broader conclusion that no single method dominates. However, the phrasing 'leads to limited improvements, if any' suggests near-uniform failure, while the prior text notes that DANN performs 'generally best' among these methods—implying some positive signal. But without context on whether DANN's gains are statistically meaningful or large in magnitude, this summary risks misrepresenting the results. The term 'generally best' could mean only marginally better than the worst. If DANN slightly outperforms others but still falls short of ERM, 'limited improvements' is fair. But if DANN matches pretraining in certain shifts, calling it 'limited' understates. The issue is not falsity but potential for misinterpretation: the reader might conclude domain generalization methods are universally weak, while the data may show niche success. The authors correctly avoid claiming superiority over ERM, but the framing could lead to an overly pessimistic takeaway.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of Dataset Acronym in Figure 2 Caption
comments.quote: DSPRITES. MPI3D. SHAPES3D. SMALLNORB. CAMELYON17. IWILDCAM.
comments.explanation: Noting that this is a figure caption, OCR issues or formatting are possible. However, the use of 'IWILDCAM' (without space) differs from typical citation of 'iWildCam' in the literature (e.g., 'iWildCam2021'). The capitalization and spelling 'IWILDCAM' is inconsistent with standard naming conventions, which use 'iWildCam' to reflect 'in camera' (image taken by trail cameras). While minor, and unlikely to mislead in context (the dataset is well-known), it is an instance of nonstandard notation. Given that figure captions are often copied into other works, maintaining correct naming is important. This may be a formatting choice, but since the text elsewhere writes 'IWILDCAM', it appears to be a stylistic inconsistency rather than an OCR error.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper presents a rigorous and highly methodical investigation into the robustness of machine learning models against distribution shifts, addressing a critical gap in the literature regarding the "why" and "when" of generalization failure. The manuscript is characterized by its significant empirical scale—training over 85,000 models—which provides a level of statistical confidence rarely seen in domain generalization benchmarks. The primary strength of the work lies in its conceptual framework: by grounding the study in latent factor disentanglement, the authors successfully move beyond the "black-box" benchmarking approach, replacing it with a principled taxonomy of failure modes (spurious correlation, low-data drift, and unseen data shift). However, a potential weakness to watch for is the "inconsistency" of the results; while the authors avoid the pitfalls of over-claiming, the paper struggles to provide a unified theory or a prescriptive takeaway, leaving the reader with a nuanced but fragmented landscape of performance rather than a cohesive solution. Provided the methodology in the full paper justifies the selection of these 19 methods and the specific datasets used to represent these factors, this stands as a high-quality, foundational contribution to the field.

comments.title: OCR and formatting artifacts affecting mathematical clarity
comments.quote: Multiple instances including definitions of Risk, Integral bounds, and weighting normalization.
comments.explanation: Several issues were identified regarding garbled mathematical notation (e.g., the definition of empirical risk $R$, the 'R' symbol for integration, and the normalized reweighting denominator). These represent systematic OCR/processing failures that obscure foundational definitions and equations necessary for technical evaluation.

comments.title: Contradictory definitions of marginal distribution shifts
comments.quote: We assume that distribution shifts arise when a new marginal distribution for the attributes is chosen, such as p(y) = p_train(y) = p_test(y)...
comments.explanation: The text states that distribution shifts occur due to new marginals, but asserts an equality that would define them as identical. This undermines the mathematical definition of the shifts being studied.

comments.title: Ambiguous/incorrect definition of nuisance attribute exclusion
comments.quote: Here, all nuisance attributes y[k] with k = l are ignored
comments.explanation: The text incorrectly references 'k = l' for nuisance attributes, where 'l' is the target label. Context implies 'k ≠ l'. Correcting this is critical to understanding which variables the model uses for risk minimization.

comments.title: Mathematical and conceptual inconsistency in shift decomposition
comments.quote: The three shifts we consider control these terms independently... The composition of these terms describes any distribution shift.
comments.explanation: The authors claim independent control over terms that are mathematically coupled in the marginal $p(y_l)$ and extend this result to 'any distribution shift' without properly qualifying reliance on the 'fixed conditional generative model' assumption. This overstates the paper’s scope.

comments.title: Incomplete formal definitions
comments.quote: UDS arises when some values in the set A [a] of an attribute y[a] are unseen in p train but are in p test:
comments.explanation: The text introduces formal concepts (like Unseen Data Shift) with a colon but fails to provide the formal mathematical expression promised. While intuition is provided, the professional standard for this analysis requires formal definitions.

comments.title: Editorial oversights in prose and structure
comments.quote: Fragmented sentences and incomplete bullet points throughout text.
comments.explanation: Multiple instances of disjointed text (e.g., '- tive' in the contribution list, broken introductory sentences) hinder the flow and clarity of the paper's core claims.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: This paper presents a rigorous and highly methodical investigation into the robustness of machine learning models against distribution shifts, addressing a critical gap in the literature regarding the "why" and "when" of generalization failure. The manuscript is characterized by its significant empirical scale—training over 85,000 models—which provides a level of statistical confidence rarely seen in domain generalization benchmarks. The primary strength of the work lies in its conceptual framework: by grounding the study in latent factor disentanglement, the authors successfully move beyond the "black-box" benchmarking approach, replacing it with a principled taxonomy of failure modes (spurious correlation, low-data drift, and unseen data shift). However, a potential weakness to watch for is the "inconsistency" of the results; while the authors avoid the pitfalls of over-claiming, the paper struggles to provide a unified theory or a prescriptive takeaway, leaving the reader with a nuanced but fragmented landscape of performance rather than a cohesive solution. Provided the methodology in the full paper justifies the selection of these 19 methods and the specific datasets used to represent these factors, this stands as a high-quality, foundational contribution to the field.

comments.title: Unexplained bullet point structure in contribution list
comments.quote: - tive and that domain generalization algorithms do work for certain datasets and distribution shifts. However, there is no easy way to select the best approach a-priori and results are inconsistent over different datasets and attributes, demonstrating there is still much work to be done to improve robustness in real-world settings.
comments.explanation: The bullet point starts mid-word ('- tive'), suggesting a significant portion of the text (likely the beginning of the sentence) was lost during the extraction or processing. While I can infer the context (likely 'We find that these methods are...,' or 'We conclude that...'), the fragment is grammatically incomplete and obscures the specific claim intended for that contribution point, which is one of the three core contributions of the paper.

comments.title: Ambiguous variable notation in empirical risk formulation
comments.quote:
Hence, we minimize the empirical risk _R_[ˆ] instead:

[section 1] where _L_ is a suitable loss function.
comments.explanation: The OCR extraction appears to have stripped the actual equation defining the empirical risk. In an academic paper, the definition of the empirical risk is foundational. While the text refers to section 1 and identifies it as R with a hat, the absence of the summation/expectation formula prevents the reader from knowing exactly how the authors define the objective (e.g., whether it is an unweighted average, or if the previously mentioned weighting factors are incorporated into the training loss).

comments.title: Fragmented sentence in introduction
comments.quote: We propose a framework to when and why we expect methods to generalise.
comments.explanation: The sentence 'We propose a framework to when and why...' is grammatically incorrect. It is likely a typo for 'We propose a framework to understand when and why...' or '...a framework for when and why...'. While the intent is clear, it reflects an editorial oversight in the introduction.

comments.title: Inconsistent Assumption on Marginal Distribution Equalities
comments.quote: We assume that distribution shifts arise when a new marginal distribution for the attributes is chosen, such as $p ( y_{[1:] [K]} ) = p_{train} ( y_{[1:] [K]} ) = p_{test} ( y_{[1:] [K]} ), but otherwise the conditional generative model is shared across all distributions
comments.explanation: The authors state that distribution shifts arise when a *new* marginal is chosen, yet immediately write an equality $p = p_{train} = p_{test}$. This is a clear contradiction. Contextually, it is obvious they mean to say that the shift is defined by the fact that $p_{train} \neq p_{test}$, and the shared component is the conditional generative model $p(x|z)$. However, as written, the text asserts the marginals are identical, which negates the definition of a distribution shift altogether.

comments.title: Confusing Notation in Integral Bounds
comments.explanation: The OCR symbol 'R' is clearly intended to represent the integral sign $\int$. While the instructions note that OCR read errors should be handled intuitively, in this specific instance the 'R' is placed immediately before a probability density/mass function without a corresponding differential (e.g., $dz$), and it is not visually distinguishable from the variable 'R' used for 'Risk' defined earlier. This could confuse a reader regarding whether this is a summation or an operator.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of Nuisance Attributes
comments.quote: Here, all nuisance attributes $y[k]$ with $k = l$ are ignored
comments.explanation: The text says 'with $k = l$ are ignored', but $y_l$ is defined as the 'label of interest' (the target variable), not a nuisance attribute. The context strongly implies the authors meant $k \neq l$. Given $y_l$ is the target, ignoring it during empirical risk minimization would be impossible (as stated later when minimizing $R$ w.r.t $y_l, x$). This is a likely typo for $k \neq l$.

comments.title: Inconsistent indicator function symbol
comments.quote: p train( y[a] = v ) ⌧ p test( y[a] = v )
comments.explanation: In standard mathematical notation, the symbol '⌧' is typically an operator for the Kronecker product or a placeholder/mis-OCR for a '≤' or '≪' (much less than) relation. Given the context of describing a 'low-data' shift where the probability in the training set is smaller than the test set, it is almost certain the authors intended a standard inequality. Since the OCR appears to have struggled with symbols in this region, this is an ambiguity that could confuse a reader trying to formalize the shift definition.

comments.title: Ambiguous/incomplete mathematical definition for Unseen Data Shift
comments.quote: UDS arises when some values in the set A [a] of an attribute y[a] are unseen in p train but are in p test:
comments.explanation: The text ends with a colon but fails to provide the promised formal equation defining the Unseen Data Shift (UDS). While the intuition is clear from the subsequent text, the structural lead-in implies a formal definition is forthcoming in the paper (likely an equation requiring that the support of the distribution at train time is a strict subset of the test time support for that attribute). Leaving a colon at the end of a sentence that introduces a formal definition without actually presenting it is a formal oversight.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in marginal distribution decomposition
comments.quote: p( y[l] ) =[P] y[a][ p][(] _[y][l][|][y][a]_[)] _[p]_[(] _[y][a]_[)][.][The][three] shifts we consider control these terms independently
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the three shifts control these terms 'independently'. However, earlier the text states that in spurious correlation, the label distribution is influenced by the nuisance attribute. If p(y[l] | y[a]) changes (as it does in Spurious Correlation), and p(y[a]) changes (as it does in Low-Data/Unseen Shift), these components are not being controlled 'independently' in a mathematical sense, as both parameters of the product affect the marginal p(y[l]). This phrasing could mislead a reader into thinking the shifts are orthogonal or separable in parameter space when they are actually conflated in the observed marginal label distribution.

comments.title: Inconsistent indexing notation in marginal distribution decomposition
comments.explanation: In Section 2.2 under 'Discussion', the marginal distribution of the label is decomposed into a sum over attribute values $y_{[a]}$. While the equation is logically grounded in the law of total probability, the notation $y_{[a]}$ serves as a variable representing any value in the set $A_{[a]}$. However, the summation index should ideally distinguish between the variable $y_{[a]}$ and the specific values $v$ it takes, especially given that $p(y_{[l]})$ equals $\sum_{v \in A_{[a]}} p(y_{[l]} | y_{[a]} = v) p(y_{[a]} = v)$. While this is a common shorthand, in the context of a paper aiming for 'fine-grained analysis', the lack of explicit distinction between the attribute index 'a' and the value index 'v' (used in the definition of shifts 2 and 3) is a minor notation inconsistency.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'unseen data shift' definition
comments.quote: $p_{\text{train}}(y_{[a]}=v) = 0$
comments.explanation: The text states that in Unseen Data Shift, 'some values in the set $A_{[a]}$ ... are unseen in $p_{\text{train}}$ but are in $p_{\text{test}}$.' Then it defines this as $p_{\text{train}}(y_{[a]}=v) = 0$. However, the introduction note states: 'Unseen data shift assumes a subset of attribute values are observed during training to facilitate the learning of invariance.' This suggests that while *some* values are unseen, *others* must be seen to allow for learning. The definition is slightly underspecified regarding the lower bound of the support of $p_{\text{train}}(y_{[a]})$. While the context allows the reader to infer it is not an empty set, it is a point of potential ambiguity for the formal definition of the shift.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in latent factor distribution
comments.explanation: In Section 2.1, the authors introduce the notation y_[1:] _[K]_ to represent the set of attributes. However, earlier in the same section, they define this set as y_[1] , y_[2] , . . . , y[K] and use the shorthand y_[1:] _[K]_ elsewhere. The subscript '[K]' in the expression 'y_[1:] _[K]_' is redundant and syntactically confusing when placed outside the range notation. While the intent is clearly to denote the sequence of attributes from 1 to K, the notation index is inconsistent within the paper's own definition.

comments.title: Underspecified Definition of 'p_reweight' denominator
comments.quote: W ( y_[1:] _[K]_ ) = p_ ( y_[1:] _[K]_ ) /p_ train( y_[1:] _[K]_ ). This means that given the attributes, the i-th data point ( yi_[1:] _[K] , x i ) in the training set is used with probability W ( yi_[1:] _[K]_ ) / [P] _[n] i[0]_ =1 _[W]_[(] _[y] i_[1:] _[0][K]_ )

While it is standard to normalize importance weights, the expression is mathematically ambiguous. It is clear they intend to normalize the weights to sum to one, but the notation provided is inconsistent with the preceding definition of W, and the indexing (i=0 to n) with the OCR-corrupted notation makes for an imprecise definition of the reweighting procedure.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'The composition of these terms' claim
comments.quote: The three shifts we consider control these terms independently: unseen data shift and low-data drift control p( y[a] ) whereas spurious correlation controls p( y[l] |y[a] ). The composition of these terms describes any distribution shift for these two variables.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that these three shifts are 'building blocks' and their composition describes 'any distribution shift'. This is a strong claim. While they show that p(y[l]) can be factored into p(y[l]|y[a]) and p(y[a]), they do not address shifts in the conditional p(x|y). Since the paper assumes p(x|y) is invariant, this claim is technically only true under their specific 'latent factor' assumption. progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper delivers a substantial and empirically rigorous contribution to the study of distribution shifts, distinguished by its impressive scale of evaluation (training over 85K models across 19 methods) and a principled framework based on latent factorization that effectively disentangles the mechanisms driving generalization failure. The narrative is compelling, successfully contextualizing the tension between recent pessimistic assessments of domain generalization and the practical success of modern techniques by arguing that progress exists but is highly sensitive to the fine-grained nature of the shift, with pretraining and augmentation emerging as dominant factors. However, the manuscript currently suffers from significant presentation flaws that impede clarity; the mathematical notation contains pervasive formatting errors (e.g., `y[k] 2 A_k` likely intended as `y[k] \in A_k`, garbled subscripts, and a fragmented sentence in the contributions list starting with "tive"), which undermine the professional polish expected at this level. Furthermore, while the claim of contradicting Gulrajani & Lopez-Paz (2021) is provocative, the authors must exercise caution in framing the results to ensure readers understand that the observed gains may stem largely from the efficacy of standard techniques like pretraining rather than advanced domain generalization algorithms, necessitating a nuanced discussion on the boundary between improved baselines and novel DG mechanisms. Immediate attention to proofreading, notation consistency, and the precise framing of comparative claims is required to fully realize the paper's high potential.

comments.title: Contradictory equality in distribution shift definition
comments.quote: We assume that distribution shifts arise when a new marginal distribution for the attributes is chosen, such as _p_ ( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) = _p_ train( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ) = _p_ test( _y_[1:] _[K]_ ), but otherwise the conditional generative model is shared across all distributions
comments.explanation: The text states that distribution shifts arise when a new marginal distribution is chosen, but immediately follows with an equality chain implying $p(y_{1:K}) = p_{\text{train}}(y_{1:K}) = p_{\text{test}}(y_{1:K})$. This equality directly contradicts the premise of a distribution shift (where $p_{\text{test}}$ and $p_{\text{train}}$ must differ). The most plausible intended statement is $p_{\text{test}}(y_{1:K}) \neq p_{\text{train}}(y_{1:K})$ or a similar strict inequality. As written, the equation negates the core concept the framework is built upon.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in augmented distribution formula
comments.quote: More precisely, we can generate data from the augmented distribution ˆ _p_ aug = (1 _− ↵_ ) _p_ reweight + _↵p_ ( _**x** |y_[1:] _[K]_ ) _p_ ( _y_[1:] _[K]_ )
comments.explanation: In the preceding sentences, the authors introduce a learned conditional generative model denoted as $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}|y_{1:K})$ and state that it is learned from training data. However, the mixture formula for the augmented distribution $\tilde{p}_{\text{aug}}$ switches to the true, unknown distribution $p(\mathbf{x}|y_{1:K})$. Using the true data-generating conditional defeats the purpose of the method described, as it requires access to ground-truth distributions unavailable in practice. The formula should consistently use the learned estimate $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}|y_{1:K})$.

comments.title: Missing independence assumption and corrupted operator in representation learning factorization
comments.quote: An alternative factorization of a data generating distribution (e.g. train) is _p_ train( _y_[1:] _[K] ,_ _**x**_ ) = R _p_ ( _z|_ _**x**_ ) _p_ train( _y_[1:] _[K] |z_ ) _dz_ . / p_ ( _**x** |z_ ) _p_ ( _z_ )
comments.explanation: The factorization $p_{\text{train}}(y_{1:K}, \mathbf{x}) = \int p(z|\mathbf{x}) p_{\text{train}}(y_{1:K}|z) dz$ is mathematically valid only if $y_{1:K} \perp \mathbf{x} \mid z$ (i.e., the attributes are conditionally independent of the raw inputs given the latent representation $z$). This is a standard disentanglement assumption, but the authors do not explicitly state it. Additionally, a related expression in the same derivation uses a corrupted operator (`/`), which is almost certainly an OCR artifact breaking the application of Bayes' rule or proportionality. Both the missing assumption and the corrupted symbol require correction for mathematical rigor.

comments.title: Non-standard notation and unjustified directional inequality in spurious correlation definition
comments.explanation: The subscript notation `y[b][−]_[1]` is non-standard and syntactically odd, deviating from conventional notation like $y_{-b}$ or $y_{\setminus b}$ for excluding an attribute from a tuple. Furthermore, the strict greater-than inequality ($>$) used to define spurious correlation implicitly assumes a positive correlation without justification. A general, mathematically rigorous definition should use $\neq$ or explicitly state the assumed direction of correlation to remain valid for arbitrary shift directions.

comments.title: Missing mathematical formulation for Shift 3 definition
comments.quote:
In our framework, _unseen data shift_ arises when some values in the set A _[a]_ of an attribute _y[a]_ are unseen in _p_ train but are in _p_ test:

This is a stronger constraint than in standard out-of-distribution generalization (see section 6), as multiple values for A _[a]_ must be seen under _p_ train, which allows the model to learn invariance to _y[a]_ .
comments.explanation: The colon at the end of the sentence strongly implies that a formal mathematical definition should follow, mirroring the structure of Shifts 1 and 2 (which both include explicit probability inequalities). The text instead jumps directly to the next sentence. A standard formulation would be something like $p_{\text{train}}(y[a]=v)=0$ and $p_{\text{test}}(y[a]=v)>0$. This omission leaves the definition underspecified and forces readers to infer the exact formal condition, which could lead to ambiguous implementation choices when reproducing the framework.

comments.title: Notation reuse in label noise definition
comments.quote: _y[i] ⇠ c_ ( _y[i]_ ), where _y[i] 2_ A _[i]_ is the true label, _y_ ˆ _[i] 2_ A _[i]_ the corrupted, observed one, and _c_ the corrupting function.
